moisture.title: Moisture Balance
moisture.resultIncrease: The RH in the greenhouse will increase.
moisture.resultDecrease: The RH in the greenhouse will decrease.
moisture.resultStable: The RH in the greenhouse will remain stable.

intro.title: Welcome to the Greenhouse Psychrometric Calculator
intro.p1: This tool computes psychrometric properties of air across four environmental zones in a greenhouse: Outside, Above Screen, Inside, and Plant surface.
intro.p2: It visualizes conditions on an interactive psychrometric chart (Mollier diagram) and provides energy balance, moisture balance, and moisture control calculations specific to greenhouse operations.
intro.p3: Designed for greenhouse growers, climate engineers, and horticultural consultants operating in hot and arid climates where managing temperature, humidity, and energy is critical for crop health.
intro.step1: Psychro Diagram — Adjust temperature and humidity for each zone. View computed properties and differences between zones.
intro.step2: Geo Location — Find your greenhouse location on the map and load current weather data.
intro.step3: Energy Balance — Calculate required ventilation rate based on solar radiation and heat loss.
intro.step4: Moisture Balance — Determine whether humidity will increase or decrease in your greenhouse.
intro.step5: Moisture Control — Size fans and calculate moisture transport through the crop canopy.
intro.extraNote: Click "Show Extra" in the top-right corner to reveal the Energy Balance, Moisture Balance, and Moisture Control tabs.

info.ah: Absolute Humidity (Mixing Ratio): Grams of water vapor per kilogram of dry air. Higher values mean more moisture in the air. Critical for understanding actual moisture content independent of temperature.
info.hd: Humidity Deficit: The difference between the maximum moisture the air could hold (at saturation) and the actual moisture content. A higher deficit means the air can absorb more moisture, promoting plant transpiration.
info.h: Enthalpy: Total heat content per kg of dry air, including both sensible heat (temperature) and latent heat (moisture). Used to calculate energy requirements for ventilation.
info.vpd: Vapor Pressure Deficit: The difference between saturation and actual vapor pressure. VPD is the primary driver of plant transpiration. Optimal VPD for most greenhouse crops is 0.4–1.2 kPa.
info.vp: Vapor Pressure: The actual partial pressure exerted by water vapor in the air. Depends on both temperature and relative humidity.
info.vpsat: Saturation Vapor Pressure: The maximum vapor pressure at a given temperature. Increases exponentially with temperature — warm air can hold much more moisture.
info.tdp: Dew Point: The temperature at which air becomes saturated and condensation begins. If any surface in the greenhouse is at or below the dew point, water will condense — risking fungal disease.
